Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Deconstruct the Requirements: Input: event_log table with case_id, activity, timestamp. Step 1: Get ordered sequence of activities per case_id (ordered by timestamp). Step 2: Group cases by this sequence (this defines a variant). Step 3: Count cases per variant and find the top K variants. Step 4: Return all original events from cases that belong to these top K variants.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Step 1: Sequence per case How to create an ordered sequence of activities in DuckDB? We can use list_agg or string_agg with an ORDER BY clause. Lets use string_agg(activity, -> ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant. SELECT case_id, string_agg(activity, -> ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self-Correction/Refinement: Is it better to use ARRAY_AGG (DuckDB uses list())? list(activity ORDER BY timestamp) creates a list. Comparing lists vs strings for grouping works in DuckDB, but strings are universally easier to debug. Lets stick to string_agg as its more standard across SQL dialects and visually intuitive for variants.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 2 & 3: Count cases per variant and find top K From the above CTE, we group by variant and count case_ids. Then we order by count descending and limit to K. WITH case_variants AS ( SELECT case_id, string_agg(activity, -> ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id ), variant_counts AS ( SELECT variant, count(case_id) AS case_count FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT K )

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Step 4: Return original events for top K variants We need to join the original event_log with the cases that belong to the top K variants. We can identify the case_ids that belong to the top variants by joining case_variants with variant_counts. WITH case_variants AS (...), variant_counts AS (...), top_k_cases AS ( SELECT cv.case_id FROM case_variants cv JOIN variant_counts vc ON cv.variant = vc.variant ) SELECT e.* FROM event_log e JOIN top_k_cases tk ON e.case_id = tk.case_id ORDER BY e.case_id, e.timestamp;

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Review against constraints: Construct a DuckDB SQL query - Yes. determines the sequence of activities for each case_id, maintaining the order imposed by timestamp - Yes, ORDER BY timestamp inside string_agg. Group cases by their complete activity sequence to identify unique process variants - Yes, GROUP BY variant. Count how many cases correspond to each variant and determine the top K variants - Yes, COUNT() and LIMIT K. Return all events from the original event log that belong only to cases which are part of these top K variants - Yes, final join.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Refining the Query & DuckDB Specifics: DuckDB has very efficient USING joins and IN clauses. An IN clause or a semi-join (EXISTS or JOIN) works well. Using list might be strictly more correct if activities contain the delimiter string. But in process mining, -> is standard. Lets use a parameter placeholder for K, like K.

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Final Polish: Ensure syntax is valid DuckDB. string_agg(expression, delimiter ORDER BY sort_expression) is valid in DuckDB. Mention the delimiter edge-case and how list() can be used as an alternative to string_agg. Lets verify list with GROUP BY and JOIN: SELECT list(activity ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id DuckDB can definitely group by a list, and join on a list.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This is clean, correct, and efficiently fulfills all prompt requirements.
